coredata.dc:title: The N,S co-doped carbon dots with excellent luminescent properties from green tea leaf residue and its sensing of gefitinib
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Zhixiong
Jiao, Xin-Yue
Xu, Li
coredata.dc:description: The green tea leaf residue was used as the carbon source to produce carbon dots (named as T-CDs) by combining pyrolyzation at high temperature and oxidation by concentrated H2SO4. Owing to the co-doping of S and N, T-CDs possessed high fluorescence quantum yield up to 14.8%. The fluorescence of the T-CDs was stable to the environmental pH, ionic strength and ultraviolet radiation, but sensitive to temperature with switchable intensity between 15 °C and 65 °C. The fluorescence of T-CDs was also found to be quenched by gefitinib via the inner-filter effect. Thus, T-CDs were used as the fluorescence sensor for gefitinib determination with good linearity in the range of 0.1 μg/mL to 20 μg/mL with satisfactory accuracy. The proposed T-CDs were obtained based on waste recycling of tea leaf residue for sustainability, and this strategy widened the applications of CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tea leaf residue
Carbon dots
Fluorescence quench
Gefitinib

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of T-CDs from tea leaf residue
Determination of gefitinib in urine samples using T-CDs as fluorescent sensor
Fluorescence sensing of gefitinib in urine sample

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Key Laboratory for Deep Processing of Major Grain and Oil

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Education of the People's Republic of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The green tea leaf (Wufeng, YiChang, Hubei, China) used in this study was purchased from a local tea market. The ultrapure water was collected from a Heal Fore NW system (Shanghai, China). Concentrated H 2 SO 4 , NaOH, NaCl, KCl, sodium phosphate monobasic, dibasic sodium phosphate, trisodium phosphate anhydrous, acetone, urea and oxalic acid (OA) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Uric acid (UA) was obtained from Alfa Aesar Chemical Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). L-Aspartic acid (L-Asp), L-glutamic acid (L-Glu), L-isoleucine (L-Iso), L-cysteine (L-Cys), L-phenylalanine (L-Phe), L-tryptophane (L-Try), anhydrous citric acid (CA) and gefitinib were supported by Aladdin Industrial Corporation. Ascorbic acid (AA) was provided by Keyuan Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China).

The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of this research by the Open Fund of Key Laboratory for Deep Processing of Major Grain and Oil ( Wuhan Polytechnic University ), Ministry of Education ( 2018JYBQGDKFA02 ) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 2017KFYXJJ149 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optimization of synthetic conditions. The effect of (a) pyrolysis temperature of tea powders, (b) oxidation duration of carbonized tea ashes in concentrated H2SO4 and (c) proportion of carbonized residue to concentrated H2SO4 on the φT-CD.
XPS spectra of the T-CDs. (a) Survey spectrum of XPS. High-resolution spectra: (b) C1s; (c) N1s; (d) O1s; (e) S2p.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of T-CDs when excited ranging from 320 nm to 400 nm. (b) Maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of T-CDs and the absorbance spectrum of gefitinib. (c) Fluorescence decay curve of T-CDs. (d) UV–Vis absorbance of T-CDs. Inset: photographs of T-CDs solution under 365 nm UV light (left) and natural light (right).

The variation of fluorescence intensity of T-CDs under the different conditions: (a) different pHs ranging from 1 to 12; (b) ionic strength from 0 M to 1 M; (c) UV irradiation for 3 h; (d) the environmental temperature from 10 °C to 60 °C. (e) temperature ranging from 15 °C to 65 oC for 5 cycles.
Emission spectra of T-CDs in the presence of gefitinib of different concentrations.
Influences on the fluorescence quenching (F/F0) of T-CDs by gefitinib: (a) concentration of T-CDs; (b) sample pH from 2 to 8; (c) incubation time; (d) some interfering substances.

head.ce:title: The N,S co-doped carbon dots with excellent luminescent properties from green tea leaf residue and its sensing of gefitinib

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Deep Processing of Major Grain and Oil (Wuhan Polytechnic University), Ministry of Education, Wuhan 430023, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Deep Processing of Major Grain and Oil (Wuhan Polytechnic University)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aKey Laboratory for Deep Processing of Major Grain and Oil (Wuhan Polytechnic University), Ministry of Education, Wuhan 430023, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tongji School of Pharmacy, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430030, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bTongji School of Pharmacy, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430030, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two authors contribute to this work equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The green tea leaf residue was used as the carbon source to produce carbon dots (named as T-CDs) by combining pyrolyzation at high temperature and oxidation by concentrated H2SO4. Owing to the co-doping of S and N, T-CDs possessed high fluorescence quantum yield up to 14.8%. The fluorescence of the T-CDs was stable to the environmental pH, ionic strength and ultraviolet radiation, but sensitive to temperature with switchable intensity between 15 °C and 65 °C. The fluorescence of T-CDs was also found to be quenched by gefitinib via the inner-filter effect. Thus, T-CDs were used as the fluorescence sensor for gefitinib determination with good linearity in the range of 0.1 μg/mL to 20 μg/mL with satisfactory accuracy. The proposed T-CDs were obtained based on waste recycling of tea leaf residue for sustainability, and this strategy widened the applications of CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent nanomaterials, such as semiconductor quantum dots [1], graphene quantum dots [2] and carbon dots (CDs) [3], have drawn great attention in recent years due to their wide applications in multi-fields of chemistry and biomedicine. Thanks to the quantum confinement, these fluorescent nanoparticles possessed superior fluorescence properties to the traditional fluorescent dyes, e.g. fluorescein and rhodamine 6G, in fluorescent labeling and imaging. However, semiconductor quantum dots were normally regarded as unfriendliness to the environment and organisms [4] on account of the toxicity caused by doping of heavy metals; as a result, the applications in vivo and in vitro were severely limited. Alternatively, CDs are winning their popularity [5–7], owing to their excellent fluorescence performance, good water solubility and biocompatibility.

CDs were a new kind of zero-dimensional carbon nanoparticles, firstly originated from single-walled carbon nanotubes [8], and formally named as carbon dots in 2006 [9]. The CDs were mainly synthesized based on top-down or bottom-up methods [6,7]. In general, laser ablation, electrochemical method and arc-discharge approach were the top-down methods; the fluorescence quantum yields of the CDs prepared by these approaches were not more than 12% [6]. By contrast, bottom-up approaches, which included oxidation by concentrated acid, microwave-assisted and hydrothermal approaches, were deemed to operate conveniently, resulting in the higher quantum yields and better fluorescence properties than those of top-down methods [6]. In addition, as for the carbon source, both the natural source and chemicals could be the choice. Considering the “green” nature of the natural source, it seems that natural substances treated with bottom-up methods could be a superior synthetic choice.

In this study, the green tea leaf residue was used as carbon source, and the bottom-up route combining pyrolyzation at high temperature and oxidation by concentrated H2SO4 was adopted to prepare fluorescent CDs (named as T-CDs). The hydrothermal treatment of oolong tea [10] and black tea [11] was reported to get CDs. However, the fluorescence quantum yields were not high, about 4.9%, possibly because both of these CDs were only N-doping. Besides, the direct usage of tea leaf as the carbon source was a waste of natural resources from the perspective of resource conservation. Herein, the green tea leaf residue was adopted as the carbon source, which was waste recycling. In addition, since, unlike the oolong tea and black tea, there is no fermentation in the production process, green tea contains polyphenols and vitamins [12]. Thus, some different functionalities could emerge on the T-CDs, and endow CDs different fluorescence properties. Particularly, as demonstrated in our study, N and S co-doping was observed in the T-CDs deriving from green tea leaf residue, with high fluorescence quantum yield up to 14.8%, which would be beneficial to enhance the sensing sensitivity.

Gefitinib is a small-molecule tyrosine kinase inhibitor and inhibits the growth of tumor cells by blocking the epidermal growth factor receptor signaling pathway. It is effective for the treatment of non-small-cell lung cancer, alleviating symptoms rapidly and prolonging the life of survival significantly [13]. For quality control of the related drugs and in-vivo studies, several methods, mainly including high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [14] and liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [15], were developed. However, it was seldom to use the fluorescent sensor for its determination. The fluorescent sensor was merited with simple and fast operation. Since the excitation spectrum of T-CDs was partly overlapped with the absorbance spectrum of gefitinib, T-CDs were herein attempted as the fluorescent sensor for gefitinib determination by inner-filter effect.

The green tea leaf (Wufeng, YiChang, Hubei, China) used in this study was purchased from a local tea market. The ultrapure water was collected from a Heal Fore NW system (Shanghai, China). Concentrated H2SO4, NaOH, NaCl, KCl, sodium phosphate monobasic, dibasic sodium phosphate, trisodium phosphate anhydrous, acetone, urea and oxalic acid (OA) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Uric acid (UA) was obtained from Alfa Aesar Chemical Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). L-Aspartic acid (L-Asp), L-glutamic acid (L-Glu), L-isoleucine (L-Iso), L-cysteine (L-Cys), L-phenylalanine (L-Phe), L-tryptophane (L-Try), anhydrous citric acid (CA) and gefitinib were supported by Aladdin Industrial Corporation. Ascorbic acid (AA) was provided by Keyuan Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China).

The morphology of the T-CDs was observed by a JEM-2100 high resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) (JEOL, Japan), and the photographs were captured by an ORIUS™ SC 1000 CCD camera. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was obtained on an AVATAR 360 Transform Infrared spectrometer (Thermo, USA). The fluorescence spectra were recorded on an F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The fluorescence decay curve was recorded on an FELIX32 Fluorescence Lifetime spectrometer (Photon Technology International, USA). The UV–Vis spectra were obtained from a UV-3600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were recorded on an ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo, USA) and deconvoluted by XPS PEAK software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of T-CDs from tea leaf residue
ce:section.ce:para: The tea leaf residue was collected after the tea infusion was drank up, dried at 80 °C and ground into powder by a household crusher. The tea leaf powder was then calcinated at 350 °C for 2 h under the air condition. The black carbonized residue (1 g) was then immersed in concentrated H2SO4 (5 mL) for 20 h oxidation and carbonization. Afterwards, NaOH solution was added to adjust the pH of the reaction mixture to neutral, followed by centrifuging at 4000 rpm (2862×g) to remove the large particles. Acetone was added to the supernatant to precipitate the sulfates that co-existed with T-CDs; it was repeated several times to remove sulfates thoroughly. Afterwards, the supernatant was collected and concentrated by rotary evaporation, and further dried in an oven, producing white powder, i.e. T-CDs. In order to obtain high fluorescent T-CDs, synthesis conditions, such as pyrolysis temperature of tea leaf powder, oxidation time duration and the proportion of the black residue to concentrated H2SO4, were optimized one by one.

The measurement and calculation of fluorescence quantum yield (φ) referred to the previous report [16]. For measurement, quinine sulfate (a standard substance, φ = 0.54 in the presence of 0.1 M H2SO4) and T-CDs aqueous solution were diluted to a concentration gradient (the absorbance of all the solution should be less than 0.1 to reduce self-absorption), respectively. The absorbance values at 360 nm and the integrated fluorescence intensities at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm were recorded. Two fitting curves for quinine sulfate and T-CDs were obtained, respectively, in which the absorbance and the integrated fluorescence intensities were x-axis and y-axis, respectively. The slopes for quinine sulfate and CDs in the fitting curves, i.e. Ks and Kx in Eq. (I), were also obtained.
(I)
φ x
=
φ s
(
K x
/
K s
)
(
η x
/
η s
)
2
x stands for T-CDs; s stands for quinine sulfate; η represents the refractive index of solvents. Herein, 0.1 M H2SO4 and water both with η of 1.33 are solvents for quinine sulfate and T-CDs, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of gefitinib in urine samples using T-CDs as fluorescent sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The human urine sample was centrifuged for 3 min at 4000 rpm (2862 × g) to remove impurities firstly, and isometric volume of methanol was then added to precipitate protein. The supernatant was collected after centrifugation, and standard gefitinib solutions were then spiked to the blank urine sample. Twenty microliter of T-CDs solution (12.8 mg/mL), 980 μL of PBS (pH 4.0, 20 mM) and 1 mL gefitinib spiked urine samples were mixed together, reaching the final gefitinib concentrations of 0.1 μg/mL, 0.25 μg/mL, 1 μg/mL, 2 μg/mL, 4 μg/mL, 7.5 μg/mL, 9 μg/mL, 10 μg/mL, 18 μg/mL and 20 μg/mL, respectively. After a 10 min-incubation at room temperature, the mixture was subjected to the fluorescence measurement. The spectrum of each mixture was scanned from 370 nm to 600 nm at the excitation wavelength of 320 nm and the fluorescence intensity was recorded at the emission wavelength of 410 nm.

To study the selectivity of this fluorescent sensor, some possible interferences in urine, such as amino acids (L-Asp, L-Glu, L-Iso, L-Cys, L-Phe and L-Try), urea, UA, CA, AA, OA, NaCl, KCl and Na3PO4 (1 mL, 20 μg/mL), were individually added to the T-CDs solution. The fluorescence intensity ratio of each sample to the blank (F/F0, F and F0 represent fluorescence intensity of T-CDs in the presence and absence of gefitinib, respectively.) was an indicator to estimate the selectivity of the sensor.

Fig. 1 (a)–(c) depicted the effect of three synthetic conditions, i.e. pyrolysis temperature of tea leaf powder, oxidation time duration and the proportion of the black residue to the concentrated H2SO4, on the φT-CDs (φ of the T-CDs). Fig. 1(a) depicted the variation trend of φT-CDs with the pyrolysis temperature (250 °C to 400 °C) of tea leaf powder. The φT-CDs increased gradually from 250 °C to 350 °C and sharply decreased at 400 °C. The plausible explanation was that tea leaf powder was carbonized into small organic molecules at a relatively high temperature, such as 350 °C; however, higher pyrolysis temperature than that, e.g. 400 °C, may cause the great loss of the organics in the tea leaf powder [14], lowering quantum yield. Additionally, without pyrolysis, the obtained yellow solution exhibited no obvious fluorescence (φ was only 0.2%). It was speculated that concentrated H2SO4 was insufficient to carbonize tea leaf powder into small organic molecules, leading to the failure in the formation of T-CDs. Therefore, pyrolysis was necessary to yield T-CDs and 350 °C was optimal.

In our preliminary experiment, when the black carbonized residue was directly dispersed in ultrapure water without treatment by concentrated H2SO4, φT-CDs was calculated to be 4.8%, which was much lower than that obtained by treating with concentrated H2SO4. Hence, the oxidation was a crucial step to enhance the quantum yield, and thus adopted in our experiment. The influence of oxidation time duration on the φT-CDs is shown in Fig. 1(b). The φT-CDs reached its maximum at 20 h oxidation. Dehydration and oxidation were expected during this course, leading to the formation of C = C, COH and -COOH [17] as demonstrated by the following characterizations. Nevertheless, φT-CDs decreased along with the further extension of oxidation time, which may be because the chemical bond inside the T-CDs was damaged by concentrated H2SO4.

Fig. 1(c) indicates that the proportion of the black residue to concentrated H2SO4 of 1 g: 5 mL was the optimal. The internal structure of T-CDs may be destroyed in the case of excessive concentrated H2SO4 while less concentrated H2SO4 was insufficient to oxidize black residue [18].

In summary, 2 h of pyrolyzation at 350 °C, followed by 20 h of oxidation with a proportion of 1 g black residue to 5 mL concentrated H2SO4 were chosen as the optimal synthetic conditions. In this way, the φT-CD up to 14.8% was obtained. Generally, fluorescence quantum yields of CDs from natural carbon sources were lower than those from chemical sources. Herein, compared to the reported CDs from other natural carbon sources, e.g. oolong tea (4.9%) [10], banana juice (8.95%) [19], bombyx mori silk (13.9%) [20], tomato juice (13.9%) [21], mango peel (8.5%) [22], konjac flour but passivated by PEG (13% in distilled water and 22% in ethanol) [23], pomelo peel (6.9%) [24], watermelon peel (7.1%) [25] and lemon peel (14%) [26], the φT-CD was relatively in a high level. As discussed above, this could be due to the oxidation and carbonization process involved to generate T-CDs in addition to the characteristic ingredients of the green tea leaf.

T-CDs looked spherical in appearance and had the uniform diameter with the average size of 2 nm, as displayed in Fig. 2 . From the inset of Fig. 2, no obvious lattice was observed in the high-resolution image, which demonstrates that the T-CDs excluded the structure of graphite carbon, which was consistent with some previous literatures [18,27].

FT-IR spectrum of T-CDs is exhibited in Fig. 3 . The broad characteristic peak centering at 3454 cm−1 was attributed to OH and/or NH bond. Two weak peaks at 1633 cm−1 and 1417 cm−1 corresponded to the C = O/C = C bonds and CN bond, respectively. A strong and sharp characteristic peak at 1138 cm−1 could be assigned to the CO/CN/C-S bonds. The FT-IR results manifested the possible existence of hydrophilic groups on the surface of T-CDs, such as carboxyl, carbonyl, hydroxyl and amino groups, explaining their excellent water solubility. In addition, these hydrophilic groups and the elements of N and S co-doping in T-CDs enhanced the φT-CDs effectively [28].

Fig. 4 exhibits the survey XPS spectrum of T-CDs and the deconvolution results of C1s, N1s, O1s and S2p spectra. In Fig. 4(a), four peaks, which located at 285 eV (40.83%), 399 eV (2.15%), 531 eV (46.1%) and 169 eV (10.92%), were clearly identified, corresponding to the binding energy of C1s, N1s, O1s and S2p [29], respectively. This demonstrates that the T-CDs contained elements of C, N, O and S, which was in accordance with the FT-IR results. High-resolution spectrum of C1s in Fig. 4(b) was fitted into four peaks, i.e. 284.5 eV, 285.0 eV, 285.3 eV and 288.2 eV, which were speculated to be related to CC/C = C, CN/C-S, CO and C = O, respectively [30]. In Fig. 4(c), the peaks at 398.8 eV and 399.6 eV were assigned to CNC and N-(C)3 bonds, respectively [31]. Three deconvolution peaks at 531.2 eV, 532.1 eV and 535.8 eV in Fig. 4(d) were identified as C = O, COC/COH and HOH bonds, respectively [30,31]. In Fig. 4(e), S2p spectrum was deconvoluted into peaks at 168.3 eV and 169.5 eV, which could be attributed to the bonds of S = O and -C-SOx- (x = 2, 3, 4), respectively [29]. The formation of CC/CN bonds in the framework may occur in the process of carbonization at high temperature pyrolysis. Other unsaturated bonds and hydrophilic functional groups may be produced by the treatment of concentrated H2SO4, which resulted in dehydration and oxidation [18].

From the above characterizations, the T-CDs were N,S co-doped. It was well known that N, S co-doping was effective to improve the fluorescence performance, as nitrogen atom had five outermost orbital electrons which could be bonded to carbon atoms stably and sulfur atom could provide the higher density of emissive trap states thus the band-gap energy was modified [28]. From this aspect, the prepared T-CDs would be promising for fluorescence sensing.

The fluorescence and absorbance spectra of T-CDs are shown in Fig. 5 . In Fig. 5(a), the fluorescence emission spectra had an obvious redshift as the excitation wavelength ranged from 320 nm to 400 nm, accompanying the decreasing fluorescence intensity. Fig. 5(b) discloses that the maximum excitation and emission wavelength of T-CDs centered at 320 nm and 410 nm, respectively. The fluorescence decay curvy of T-CDs is depicted in Fig. 5(c), and the lifetime was calculated to be 4.83 ns. In addition, as shown in Fig. 5(d), there is a weak absorption peak at 270 nm, which was attributed to the π- π* transition of C = C [26] and correlated well with the above XPS results. As photographed in the inset of Fig. 5(d), the aqueous solution of T-CDs emitted blue fluorescence under the UV light of 365 nm, while it was nearly colorless and transparent under the natural light.
The T-CDs aqueous solution exhibited an excellent stability to the buffer pH, ionic strength and ultraviolet radiation, as reflected in Fig. 6 (a)–(c).

Interestingly, the fluorescence intensity of the T-CDs was found to be sensitive to temperature, as displayed in Fig. 6(d). The fluorescence intensity was enhanced by cooling the T-CDs solution and vice versa. Particularly, the fluorescence intensity of T-CDs could be switched when the temperature was switched between 15 °C and 65 °C, as demonstrated in Fig. 6(e). As discussed previously [30,32–34], photoexcited electron relaxation could be attained via some reactions at high temperature, e.g. radiative relaxation, nonradiative relaxation, and thermally activated trapping in surface and impurity states; however, on the contrary, photoexcited electrons could have opposite performance when the temperature was lowered [30,34]. Thus temperature-dependent fluorescence behavior may be further explained by the temperature-dependent energy gap, which was renormalization of band energies by electron-phonon interactions mainly existing in our case [33]. This temperature-dependent fluorescence property of T-CDs could be potentially explored as fluorescence temperature sensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing of gefitinib in urine sample
ce:section.ce:para: In our preliminary experiments, the fluorescence quenching of the T-CDs occurred upon gefitinib was introduced. It was speculated to be resulted from the inner-filter effect [35], for there was an overlap between the absorbance spectrum of gefitinib and the excitation spectrum of T-CDs as reflected in Fig. 5(b). Additionally, the fluorescence quenching degree was enlarged with the increasing gefitinib concentration, as displayed in Fig. 7 . Hence, the T-CDs could be the sensor for gefitinib detection.

To obtain the high sensitivity, the sensing system was optimized from three aspects, i.e. concentration of T-CDs solution, pH value of the sensing system and the incubation time for gefitinib and T-CDs, as shown in Fig. 8 . As exhibited in Fig. 8(a), when the concentration of T-CDs ranged from 6.4 mg/mL to 16 mg/mL in the presence of gefitinib of the same concentration, the analytical signal responded almost the same; however, the fluorescence intensity of T-CDs was quenched inconspicuously by gefitinib at low concentration of T-CDs, such as 3.2 mg/mL. Hence, T-CDs solution of 12.8 mg/mL was chosen as the appropriate concentration. The optimal pH of the quenching system was 4.0 with the lowest F/F0, as seen in Fig. 8(b). As shown in Fig. 8(c), the fluorescence quenching reached stable after 10 min incubation, and 10 min incubation could ensure the interaction of T-CDs and gefitinib. The optimal conditions were thus as follows: 12.8 mg/mL of T-CDs incubated with gefitinib in the PBS of pH 4.0 for 10 min at room temperature.

In order to apply the method to detect gefitinib in biological samples, some possible interferences, e.g. urea, UA, OA, CA, AA, Na3PO4, NaCl, KCl and some amino acids (L-Asp, L-Glu, L-Iso, L-Cys, L-Phe and L-Try), were used to investigate the selectivity of the established sensing system. As demonstrated in Fig. 8(d), all of the studied substances at the final concentration of 20 μg/mL displayed almost no obvious fluorescence quenching towards T-CDs except gefitinib. Hence, the T-CDs showed high specificity to gefitinib and was suitable to determine gefitinib in urine samples.

For gefitinib spiked urine samples, the concentration of gefitinib was well linear with F/F0 in the range of 0.1 μg/mL to 20 μg/mL. The regression equation was fitted to be F/F0 = −0.025C(gefitinib) + 0.9789, and the correlation coefficient was 0.9918. The recoveries of gefitinib in urine sample were 91.24% ± 13.6% (0.5 μg/mL), 106.19% ± 2.63% (5 μg/mL) and 101.93% ± 0.26% (15 μg/mL).

The developed fluorescent sensor based on T-CDs had comparable sensitivity with HPLC-UV analysis [14], which meet the requirements of the quality control analysis of the drug tablet. It was particularly merited with the fast and simple operation, suitable for the on-site detection. In addition, for a more complex sample matrix, e.g. urine herein, the sample matrix had less influence on the recovery, demonstrating its potential application to biological samples.

The present study recycled green tea leaf residue to produce the carbon dots (T-CDs) via the combination of pyrolyzation and oxidation. The fluorescence quantum yield of the T-CDs was up to be 14.8%, owing to the co-doping of N and S. The fluorescence of T-CDs was demonstrated stable to the environmental pH, ionic strength and ultraviolet radiation, but sensitive to the environmental temperature. The fluorescence of T-CDs could be quenched by gefitinib via inner-filter effect and were successfully applied to gefitinib detection in urine samples.
The raw/processed data required to reproduce these findings cannot be shared at this time due to technical or time limitations.
Zhixiong Hu: Methodology, Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition. Xin-Yue Jiao: Validation, Writing - original draft. Li Xu: Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of this research by the Open Fund of Key Laboratory for Deep Processing of Major Grain and Oil (Wuhan Polytechnic University), Ministry of Education (2018JYBQGDKFA02) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 2017KFYXJJ149).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.microc.2019.104588.
